meta.topic: The Reals via Dedekind Cuts are Uncountable

steps.narration: Our goal is to show that the real numbers, constructed via Dedekind cuts, are uncountable.
steps.content: Using Dedekind cuts, show that $\mathbb{R}$ is uncountable.
steps.label: Goal

steps.narration: First, let's recall what a Dedekind cut is. A Dedekind cut is a partition of the rationals into two nonempty sets A and B,
steps.content: A Dedekind cut is a pair $(A, B)$ where $A \cup B = \mathbb{Q}$, $A \neq \emptyset$, $B \neq \emptyset$,
steps.label: Definition

steps.narration: where every element of A is less than every element of B,
steps.content: every element of $A$ is less than every element of $B$,

steps.narration: and A has no greatest element.
steps.content: and $A$ has no greatest element.

steps.narration: Here's a picture. The number line is split into two regions. Everything to the left is A, everything to the right is B. The cut itself represents the square root of two. There's no gap here — the partition of the rationals IS the real number.
Each such cut defines a real number alpha. This is how we construct the reals from the rationals.
steps.content: Each cut $(A, B)$ defines a real number $\alpha$.

steps.narration: The set of all Dedekind cuts is the real numbers.
steps.content: The set of all Dedekind cuts $\textit{is}$ $\mathbb{R}$.

steps.narration: Now we prove our theorem: the reals are uncountable.
steps.content: $\mathbb{R}$ is uncountable.
steps.label: Theorem

steps.narration: Assume, for the sake of contradiction, that the reals are countable.
steps.content: Assume for contradiction that $\mathbb{R}$ is countable.

steps.narration: Then we could list every real number as alpha one, alpha two, alpha three, and so on.
steps.content: Then we can list all real numbers as $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \alpha_3, \dots$

steps.narration: Here is our supposed complete listing of all real numbers.
Remember, each alpha n is a Dedekind cut — a pair A n, B n partitioning the rationals.
steps.content: Each $\alpha_n$ is a Dedekind cut $(A_n, B_n)$.

steps.narration: We will now construct a new Dedekind cut, A star and B star, that cannot appear anywhere in the list.
steps.content: We will build a new cut $(A^*, B^*)$ not in the list.
steps.label: Construction

steps.narration: Our strategy: we'll construct a shrinking sequence of intervals, each one carefully chosen to dodge the next real number on the list. We're not invoking any completeness theorem here — we'll build the cut directly.
steps.content: We construct a shrinking sequence of intervals, each one dodging the next real on the list.

steps.narration: Start by picking a closed interval a one, b one with rational endpoints, chosen so that alpha one does not lie in this interval. For instance, if alpha one happens to be 2.7, we might pick the interval from 3 to 4. We can always find such an interval — divide any starting interval into thirds and pick a third that avoids alpha one.
steps.content: Pick a closed interval $[a_1, b_1]$ with $a_1, b_1 \in \mathbb{Q}$ such that $\alpha_1 \notin [a_1, b_1]$.

steps.narration: Next, pick a smaller interval a two, b two inside the first one, avoiding alpha two. Again, divide into thirds and dodge.
steps.content: Pick $[a_2, b_2] \subset [a_1, b_1]$ with $\alpha_2 \notin [a_2, b_2]$.

steps.narration: Continue this process. We get a nested sequence of closed intervals, each one inside the last, each one avoiding the next alpha on our list.
steps.content: Continue: $[a_1, b_1] \supset [a_2, b_2] \supset [a_3, b_3] \supset \cdots$

steps.narration: Notice that the left endpoints form a non-decreasing sequence — a one is at most a two, which is at most a three, and so on. They march steadily to the right.
steps.content: $a_1 \leq a_2 \leq a_3 \leq \cdots$ — the left endpoints march rightward.

steps.narration: At step n, the interval a n, b n is chosen to exclude alpha n.
steps.content: At step $n$: $\alpha_n \notin [a_n, b_n]$.

steps.narration: Now define A star as the set of all rationals q that are less than some left endpoint a n. Think of it this way: as we drill deeper with each interval, the left endpoints creep rightward. A star collects everything to the left of where they're heading.
steps.content: Define $A^* = \{ q \in \mathbb{Q} : q < a_n \text{ for some } n \}$.

steps.narration: Equivalently, A star is the union of all the sets of rationals less than a n.
steps.content: Equivalently, $A^* = \bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty} \{ q \in \mathbb{Q} : q < a_n \}$.

steps.narration: And B star is everything in the rationals not in A star.
steps.content: Let $B^* = \mathbb{Q} \setminus A^*$.

steps.narration: We claim that A star, B star is a valid Dedekind cut — that is, a real number.
steps.content: $(A^*, B^*)$ is a valid Dedekind cut.
steps.label: Claim

steps.narration: A star is nonempty because it contains a one minus one. B star is nonempty because it contains b one — since b one is at least as big as every a n, it's never less than any a n.
steps.content: $A^*$ is nonempty (contains $a_1 - 1$) and $B^*$ is nonempty (contains $b_1$).

steps.narration: Every element of A star is less than every element of B star, by construction — A star is a downward-closed set.
steps.content: Every element of $A^*$ is less than every element of $B^*$.

steps.narration: And A star has no greatest element — if q is less than a n, we can always find a rational q prime between q and a n, by density of the rationals.
steps.content: $A^*$ has no greatest element (if $q < a_n$, then $q < q' < a_n$ for some $q'$).

steps.narration: So we've built a completely legitimate real number — alpha star — directly from the nested intervals. No completeness theorem invoked, no magic. Just a union of rational sets that satisfies the three conditions of a Dedekind cut.
steps.content: So $(A^*, B^*)$ defines a real number $\alpha^*$.

steps.narration: And HERE is the punchline. Alpha star is different from every single alpha n on our list.
steps.content: $\alpha^* \neq \alpha_n$ for any $n$.
steps.label: Key Step

steps.narration: Why? Because alpha n was excluded from the interval a n, b n by construction. But alpha star lies inside every interval a n, b n — it's the real number defined by the left endpoints of all these intervals.
steps.content: Since $\alpha_n \notin [a_n, b_n]$ but $\alpha^* \in [a_n, b_n]$ for all $n$:

steps.narration: Therefore alpha star is not equal to alpha n, for any natural number n.
steps.content: \alpha^* \neq \alpha_n \quad \text{for all } n \in \mathbb{N}

steps.narration: But alpha star is a real number, and it's not anywhere in our list.
steps.content: But $\alpha^* \in \mathbb{R}$ and $\alpha^*$ is not in the list.

steps.narration: This contradicts our assumption that the list contained every real number.
steps.content: This contradicts the assumption that the list contains all of $\mathbb{R}$.

steps.narration: Therefore, the reals are uncountable.
steps.content: Therefore $\mathbb{R}$ is uncountable. $\blacksquare$

steps.narration: Let's recap. We assumed the reals were countable and listed them all. Then we constructed a Dedekind cut using nested intervals — each interval dodging the next real on the list. The resulting cut is a real number not on the list. Contradiction. The reals are uncountable.
steps.content: Assumed $\mathbb{R}$ countable $\to$ listed all reals $\to$ built a Dedekind cut dodging every entry $\to$ contradiction.
steps.label: Recap
